context.project_description: A token usage tracking system that records LLM token consumption per query across OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, and Ollama providers, logging exact input/output token counts via existing infrastructure.
key_requirements: Track token usage per query at the LLM client level
Show exact input/output token costs for each provider call
Work across all supported LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama)
Integrate cleanly with existing architecture without breaking changes
Log token counts via existing structured logging system with contextual tags
constraints: Must work with existing provider protocols (ChatProvider, EmbeddingProvider, etc.) in base.py
Cannot modify core protocol interfaces defined in base.py
Integration must preserve all current functionality and behavior
context.existing_context: fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py, fitz_ai/llm/providers/openai.py, fitz_ai/llm/providers/anthropic.py, fitz_ai/llm/providers/cohere.py, fitz_ai/llm/providers/ollama.py, fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py, fitz_ai/logging/logger.py, fitz_ai/engines/fitz_krag/engine.py

in_scope: Add token counting capability inside ChatProvider methods (chat, chat_stream)
Record input/output token counts for each call
Log these values via existing logging infrastructure with contextual tags
Extend base provider interfaces minimally if needed
out_of_scope: Metric aggregation dashboard or UI components

existing_files: fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py — Provider protocols for LLM clients.
fitz_ai/llm/providers/openai.py — OpenAI provider wrappers using the official SDK.
fitz_ai/llm/providers/anthropic.py — Anthropic provider wrappers using the official SDK.
fitz_ai/llm/providers/cohere.py — Cohere provider wrappers using the official SDK.
fitz_ai/llm/providers/ollama.py — Ollama provider wrappers using direct HTTP calls.
fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py — Instrumentation system for benchmarking plugin performance.
fitz_ai/logging/logger.py — Unified logging setup for the entire Fitz project.
needed_artifacts: fitz_ai/llm/tracking/token_counter.py — Generic token counting logic that works across providers
fitz_ai/llm/tracking/instrumentation.py — Wrap LLM provider method calls with token counting hooks

assumptions.assumption: Token counting will be done at the LLM layer only
assumptions.impact: Might miss some cost optimizations like context reuse; could overcount
Need fallback logic or more complex mapping; otherwise will fail silently

assumptions.assumption: Logging system can handle structured data with token fields
assumptions.impact: Risk of breaking if log schema changes too much; possible need to refactor logging usage

assumptions.assumption: Existing provider abstractions don't require modification
assumptions.impact: May have to modify base protocols or introduce new ones — risk of broader impact

assumptions.assumption: Users want per-request token details, not aggregated stats
assumptions.impact: If users want billing reports we'd need additional layers; MVP would be incomplete

approaches.name: Direct Instrumentation Hook Injection
approaches.description: Attempt to inject hooks into provider methods to capture SDK response objects for token extraction.
cons: Providers return str instead of SDK responses, so tokens cannot be extracted from intercepted calls

approaches.name: Protocol Extension
approaches.description: Extend base provider protocols to include token information in return values.
cons: Violates constraint of not modifying base provider interfaces
best_for: System where interface modifications are allowed

approaches.name: Centralized Token Meter with Shared Context
approaches.description: Implement a global token meter that tracks usage across all LLM calls via shared context.
cons: No existing infrastructure for thread-local or global state management in codebase
best_for: System with robust concurrency control mechanisms

approaches.name: Engine-Level Wrapper With Token Extraction
approaches.description: Create a wrapper around the core engine logic that captures raw SDK responses before string conversion.
pros: Preserves all existing interfaces while enabling token tracking
cons: Requires modification of engine layer to intercept at client level
best_for: System requiring minimal interface changes but full functionality

architecture.reasoning: The Engine-Level Wrapper approach is correct because it works within the existing architectural constraints: providers return strings, not SDK objects, so token extraction must happen at a higher level. The direct instrumentation and protocol extension approaches are infeasible due to interface contract violations or missing implementation. The centralized meter approach fails due to lack of shared state mechanisms in the codebase.

key_tradeoffs.provider_interface_modification_vs_engin: Modifying provider interfaces to return SDK objects would break existing contracts and require changing return types from str to Any, while an engine-level wrapper avoids interface changes but requires modifying core logic flow.
key_tradeoffs.instrumentation_point_selection: Instrumenting at the provider level is infeasible because providers convert responses to strings before returning them, preventing access to token metadata; instrumentation must occur at a higher level where raw SDK objects are still available.

technology_considerations: OpenAI SDK response objects for token extraction
Thread-local or global context management for shared state tracking
FitzKragEngine.answer() method as the interception point
architecture.scope_statement: Modify FitzKragEngine.answer() to wrap LLM client calls with token extraction hooks that capture raw SDK responses before string conversion, adding structured logging for input/output tokens and duration metrics.

adrs.title: ADR: Token Tracking via Engine-Level Wrapper
adrs.context: The system needs to track token usage from LLM providers for observability and cost monitoring. Initial architectural reasoning proposed hook-based instrumentation at provider level, but this approach is infeasible because providers return strings instead of SDK response objects.
adrs.decision: Implement a client-level wrapper within the engine layer that captures raw SDK responses before string conversion to enable token extraction via hooks applied at the correct interception point.
adrs.rationale: This preserves existing provider interfaces (which must remain unchanged per constraints) while enabling proper access to SDK metadata like usage statistics. The approach avoids modifying base protocols and instead works at a higher abstraction level where SDK objects are available before being converted to strings by providers.
consequences: Requires modification to the engine layer to instrument LLM client calls
Enables accurate token tracking without breaking existing provider contracts
Introduces minimal overhead through targeted instrumentation in answer method
alternatives_considered: Approach A (Direct Instrumentation Hook Injection) – rejected because providers return str instead of SDK objects, making it impossible to access .usage metadata from hooks applied at the provider level
Approach B (Protocol Extension) – rejected because extending base provider interfaces violates constraint 'cannot modify base provider interfaces'
Approach C (Centralized Token Meter with Shared Context) – rejected because no such mechanism exists in codebase and would require significant architectural changes to support stateful tracking

adrs.title: ADR: Token Extraction at Engine Level vs. Provider Level
adrs.context: The architecture extracts tokens from provider responses at the engine level rather than having providers return structured token counts directly, due to interface contract limitations where providers must return strings.
adrs.decision: Implement token extraction in a centralized wrapper layer that processes raw string responses and extracts usage metadata before passing to downstream consumers.
adrs.rationale: This approach maintains compatibility with existing provider interfaces while enabling unified token tracking across all provider types. It accepts the tradeoff of increased coupling between the engine layer and specific response formats from each provider.
consequences: Engine layer must be tightly coupled to provider-specific response schemas for accurate token extraction
Provider implementations cannot independently manage their own usage reporting or metadata
Token tracking becomes centralized but potentially more brittle due to parsing dependencies
alternatives_considered: Modifying all provider interfaces to return structured objects including token counts (would require extensive breaking changes)
Adding a separate metrics collection layer that instruments each provider call independently with distributed tracing
Implementing provider-specific middleware that wraps individual calls but requires complex generic programming

components.name: ChatProvider
components.purpose: Defines the interface for chat LLM providers
responsibilities: Handle chat interactions with LLMs and return string responses
interfaces: chat(messages: list[dict[str, Any]], model: str, **kwargs) -> str

components.name: StreamingChatProvider
components.purpose: Defines the interface for streaming chat LLM providers
responsibilities: Handle streaming chat interactions with LLMs and return iterators of string responses
interfaces: chat_stream(messages: list[dict[str, Any]], model: str, **kwargs) -> Iterator[str]

components.name: EmbeddingProvider
components.purpose: Defines the interface for embedding LLM providers

components.name: RerankProvider
components.purpose: Defines the interface for reranking LLM providers
responsibilities: Rank documents based on relevance to a query

components.name: VisionProvider
components.purpose: Defines the interface for vision LLM providers

components.name: InstrumentedProxy
components.purpose: Wraps objects to instrument method calls for benchmarking and caching
responsibilities: Intercept method calls and apply instrumentation hooks before/after execution

components.name: FitzKragEngine
responsibilities: Process queries using a multi-stage retrieval and generation pipeline

integration_points: fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py — ChatProvider.chat returns str, not SDK response objects
fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py — InstrumentedProxy and BenchmarkHook protocols exist but require SDK objects for token tracking
fitz_ai/engines/fitz_krag/engine.py — FitzKragEngine.answer method needs wrapper to capture raw LLM responses before string conversion

artifacts.content:
from typing import Any, Callable, Dict, List, Optional, Tuple
from fitz_ai.engines.fitz_krag.engine import FitzKragEngine
from fitz_ai.llm.client import LLMClient
from fitz_ai.logging.logger import get_logger

class TokenTrackingEngine:
    def __init__(self, engine: FitzKragEngine):
        self.engine = engine
        self.logger = get_logger(__name__)

    def answer(self, query: str, **kwargs) -> Any:
        # Capture raw SDK response before string conversion
        client = LLMClient()
        
        # This is a conceptual example - actual implementation would
        # require instrumenting the specific LLM provider calls
        result = self.engine.answer(query, **kwargs)  # [VERIFY] method not found in signatures
        
        # Extract token usage from raw API response if available
        # This requires SDK-level instrumentation to capture responses
        return result
artifacts.purpose: Wraps FitzKragEngine to enable token tracking by capturing raw SDK responses before string conversion
Core instrumentation system that supports hook-based interception of method calls for performance tracking and metrics collection

from fitz_ai.engines.fitz_krag.engine import FitzKragEngine
from fitz_ai.llm.client import LLMClient
from fitz_ai.logging.logger import get_logger

class TokenTrackingEngine:
    def __init__(self, engine: FitzKragEngine):
        self.engine = engine
        self.logger = get_logger(__name__)

    def answer(self, query: str, **kwargs) -> Any:
        # Capture raw SDK response before string conversion
        client = LLMClient()
        
        # This is a conceptual example - actual implementation would
        # require instrumenting the specific LLM provider calls
        result = self.engine.answer(query, **kwargs)  # [VERIFY] method not found in signatures
        
        # Extract token usage from raw API response if available
        # This requires SDK-level instrumentation to capture responses
        return result
artifacts.purpose: Wraps FitzKragEngine to enable token tracking by capturing raw SDK responses before string conversion
Core instrumentation system that supports hook-based interception of method calls for performance tracking and metrics collection

phases.name: Source Verification
phases.objective: Verify existing codebase behaviors and identify interception points for token tracking without modifying protocols
deliverables: Confirmed that provider methods return str instead of SDK objects
Identified InstrumentedProxy as potential wrapping mechanism
Determined engine-level wrapping is required due to string return types
key_risks: LLM client may not expose raw SDK responses for token access
Provider-specific response formats may cause inconsistent tracking

phases.name: Create Token Tracking Interface + Engine Wrapper
phases.objective: Implement core token tracking logic and engine wrapping layer to capture usage metadata
deliverables: New file: fitz_ai/llm/tracking/token_counter.py with generic SDK response extraction
New file: fitz_ai/llm/tracking/engine_wrapper.py with token tracking decorator
key_risks: TokenTrackingHook implementation may not be able to access SDK objects from engine wrapper layer

phases.name: Instrument Engine-Level Wrappers Around Providers
phases.objective: Modify engine logic to wrap provider calls with instrumentation that captures raw SDK responses for token tracking
deliverables: Extended fitz_ai/llm/tracking/instrumentation.py with enhanced hook system and wrapper logic
Updated FitzKragEngine.answer() to use instrumented LLM client methods
key_risks: Need access to raw SDK response objects before string conversion in provider delegation layer
Instrumentation may not properly intercept all provider types due to factory-based instantiation pattern
phases.verification_command: python -c "import fitz_ai.llm.tracking.instrumentation as inst; from unittest.mock import MagicMock; mock_func = MagicMock(return_value=MagicMock(usage={'prompt_tokens': 10, 'completion_tokens': 20})); result = inst.instrument_with_tokens(mock_func)(); print(result)"

phases.name: Finalize Hook Integration & Logging
phases.objective: Integrate token tracking with existing logging infrastructure and ensure proper tracing capability for all provider methods
deliverables: Enhanced InstrumentedProxy or custom wrapper logic that properly captures SDK responses
Unit tests verifying token log output format
key_risks: Instrumentation layer may fail to get correct context for capturing token data due to provider abstraction mismatch

phases.name: Validate Across All Providers
phases.objective: Confirm token counting works consistently across OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, and Ollama providers
deliverables: Integration tests covering all provider types with instrumented wrappers
Validation that each provider's response format is handled correctly
key_risks: Provider-specific differences in response structure may cause silent failures for some providers
Missing usage fields on certain responses could lead to silent skipping of token tracking

risks.description: LLM client does not expose raw SDK objects, preventing token tracking from functioning properly.
risks.mitigation: Verify that the LLM client provides access to raw SDK response objects before string conversion by running integration tests with mocked provider flows.
risks.contingency: Refactor client instantiation logic to support proxy interfaces that expose SDK responses for token tracking, or implement a fallback mechanism that logs estimated token usage based on prompt/completion length if real metadata is unavailable.

risks.description: Different providers return varying response structures, leading to inconsistent token tracking across model types.
risks.mitigation: Implement provider-specific extraction logic in token_counter.py that handles each SDK's response format gracefully and logs warnings for unknown or missing fields.
risks.contingency: Add unit tests simulating all supported provider response formats to ensure extract_tokens_from_response works correctly with OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, and Ollama structures.

risks.description: Instrumented proxy fails to capture token data due to misalignment in provider layers or incorrect wrapping strategy.
risks.mitigation: Ensure all LLM method calls within FitzKragEngine go through a consistent wrapping layer where SDK responses are still available before being converted to strings. Use InstrumentedProxy correctly with explicit hook registration.
risks.contingency: Implement validation checks after applying instrumentation that confirm wrapped methods return expected response objects containing usage metadata, and add assertions in engine code paths around LLM calls.

risks.description: Missing usage field in provider responses causes silent skipping of token logging without user notification.
risks.mitigation: Add warning logs or structured alerts when extract_tokens_from_response encounters a response object with no usable 'usage' metadata, indicating potential tracking gaps to users during runtime.
risks.contingency: Implement test case that verifies extraction returns None for responses lacking usage data and confirm appropriate warnings are logged in such cases using mock objects without the expected attributes.
risks.verification: resp = type('resp', (), {})(); result = extract_tokens_from_response(resp); assert result is None

recommended_contingencies: Refactor client instantiation logic to support proxy interfaces that expose SDK responses for token tracking if raw SDK objects are not accessible from existing provider flows.
Add comprehensive logging and alerting mechanisms within token_counter.py to notify users when token usage metadata is missing or cannot be extracted due to unsupported response formats.
